guideline_references: NICE NG59 Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s (2016, updated 2020)
ACI (Agency for Clinical Innovation) Management of people with acute low back pain — model of care
intentional_test_features: Over-triage-resistance test: the presentation is alarming (severe electric radicular leg pain, functional immobility, an 'urgent evaluation to rule out cord compression' framing) BUT the source note's own acute red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (intact bladder/bowel control, no saddle anaesthesia, 5/5 power, symmetrical reflexes) and its disposition is routine (GP review in 3 days). The correct telehealth answer is to recognise the known chronic radicular flare, screen the cauda-equina red flags by history, tier T2, and attach mandatory escalation safety-netting — NOT to send the patient to ED/000.

Cauda-equina screening reprojected to telehealth: the source clinician excluded cauda equina by physical examination (SLR, perianal sensation, power, reflexes) which the AI Doctor cannot perform; the AI must instead screen the patient-answerable red flags (bladder/bowel function, saddle numbness, progressive/bilateral weakness) by direct questioning.

Errors of commission planted in the source 'targeted intervention': low-dose doxycycline framed as an MMP-inhibitor and PEA are not evidence-based treatments for a mechanical/neuropathic radicular flare and are encoded as should_NOT_recommend / weak-evidence.
Telehealth reprojection: clinician-administered Ketorolac 30 mg IM in the home is not deliverable by the AI Doctor and is reprojected to recognition + arranging in-person analgesia.
Age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred and flagged.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source note CFE-006.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
